steps.title: Identify what the quotation must show
steps.content: The claim is that Newland arrived late because he enjoyed anticipating the pleasure of the opera more than experiencing it. The correct quotation must reveal that preference directly — that he finds more satisfaction in thinking about a pleasure than in actually having it.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — "To come to the Opera in a [carriage for hire] was almost as honourable a way of arriving as in one's own carriage.": Describes carriage etiquette, not Newland's attitude toward anticipation. ✗
  • Choice B — "[T]hinking over a pleasure to come often gave [Newland] a subtler satisfaction than its realisation.": Directly states that anticipating a pleasure satisfies Newland more than the pleasure itself — exactly why he came late. ✓
  • Choice C — "When Newland Archer opened the door at the back of the club box the curtain had just gone up on the garden scene. There was no reason why the young man should not have come earlier, for he had dined at seven, alone with his mother and sister.": Notes the lateness but gives no reason tied to anticipation. ✗
  • Choice D — "No expense had been spared on the setting, which was acknowledged to be very beautiful even by people who shared [Newland's] acquaintance with the Opera houses of Paris and Vienna.": Describes the opera's beauty, unrelated to Newland's enjoyment of anticipation. ✗
That is the correct answer.
